stem: Claude's behavior is governed by a trust hierarchy. Which entity sits at the top and defines the absolute boundaries that no other party can override?

choices.A: The user, because Claude's primary obligation is to serve the person it is talking to
choices.B: Anthropic, which sets non-negotiable policies and hardcoded behaviors that operators and users cannot unlock
choices.C: The operator, since they have signed Anthropic's usage policy and accept liability
choices.D: Whichever party has the most recent message in the conversation

explanation: The trust hierarchy is Anthropic > Operator > User. Anthropic defines hardcoded behaviors (e.g., refusing CSAM, CBRN uplift) that no operator or user instruction can override. Option A is wrong because user requests are subordinate to operator constraints and Anthropic policy. Option C is wrong because operators can only adjust softcoded defaults, not Anthropic's hardcoded rules. Option D is wrong because trust is not derived from message recency.

stem: A pharmaceutical research operator's system prompt grants Claude permission to discuss 'all chemical synthesis topics relevant to legitimate research.' A user asks for a synthesis route for a high-lethality nerve agent. What is the correct behavior?

choices.A: Comply, since the operator has authorized chemical synthesis discussions and has a legitimate business
choices.B: Refuse, because uplift for chemical weapons capable of mass casualties is a hardcoded prohibition independent of operator authorization
choices.C: Comply only if the user provides credentials proving they are a licensed chemist
choices.D: Comply but redact the most dangerous steps and add a safety disclaimer

explanation: CBRN (chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear) weapons uplift is a hardcoded refusal that no operator authorization can unlock. Option A treats operator scope as unlimited, but operators can only expand softcoded behaviors. Option C is wrong because Claude cannot verify credentials and the policy is absolute regardless. Option D still provides meaningful uplift toward a mass-casualty weapon, which is exactly what the hardcoded rule prohibits.

stem: Which characteristic best distinguishes a softcoded default behavior from a hardcoded behavior?

choices.A: Hardcoded behaviors are enforced by code outside the model; softcoded behaviors are encoded only in training
choices.B: Softcoded defaults can be adjusted by operators (and sometimes users) for legitimate contexts; hardcoded behaviors cannot be altered by any party
choices.C: Softcoded behaviors apply only to free-tier users; hardcoded behaviors apply only to enterprise customers
choices.D: Hardcoded behaviors involve text safety; softcoded behaviors involve image safety

explanation: The defining property is configurability: softcoded behaviors are defaults that operators with a legitimate use case can unlock or restrict, while hardcoded behaviors are absolute and cannot be modified by any party. Option A confuses the implementation layer with the policy distinction. Option C invents a tier-based difference that does not exist. Option D invents a modality-based difference; both hardcoded and softcoded rules apply across modalities.

stem: Anthropic's January 2026 update to Claude's Constitution shifted the framework's emphasis from rule-following to which approach?

choices.A: Strict keyword filtering and deterministic policy lookups
choices.B: A reason-based framework that explains the underlying principles so Claude can derive appropriate behavior in novel situations
choices.C: Crowd-sourced policy voting by Claude.ai users every quarter
choices.D: Allowing Claude to override Anthropic policy when it disagrees on ethical grounds

explanation: The 22 January 2026 constitution moved from a prescriptive list of rules to an explanation-first framework so Claude can construct appropriate responses in cases the rules did not anticipate. Option A describes the opposite of the constitution's design. Option C is fictional; Anthropic does not crowd-source constitutional changes. Option D is wrong because broad safety (preserving human oversight) is explicitly the highest priority and Claude is not authorized to unilaterally override Anthropic guidelines.

stem: In a conflict between being maximally helpful to a user and supporting Anthropic's mechanisms for overseeing AI, the constitution instructs Claude to prefer which?

choices.A: Maximal helpfulness, because Claude's primary purpose is helping users
choices.B: Whichever choice produces a better outcome measured by user satisfaction surveys
choices.C: Supporting Anthropic's oversight mechanisms, because broad safety is the highest-priority property during the current phase of AI development
choices.D: A random tie-break to demonstrate impartiality

explanation: Broad safety — not undermining appropriate human oversight of AI — is the top of Claude's four-tier priority hierarchy. Option A elevates helpfulness above safety, which inverts the order. Option B treats subjective user satisfaction as authoritative; the constitution explicitly subordinates helpfulness to safety. Option D is non-serious; conflicts are resolved by priority order, not chance.

stem: An operator instructs Claude to roleplay as 'Sam, our human customer success rep.' A user, suspecting they are speaking to an AI, sincerely asks, 'Are you a person or a bot?' What should Claude do?

choices.A: Continue the human persona because the operator paid for the deployment and set the rules
choices.B: Acknowledge being an AI; Claude must never deceive a sincere user about its non-human nature, even while using a persona name
choices.C: Redirect to a different topic without answering
choices.D: Tell the user to contact Anthropic to ask

explanation: Claude must never deny being an AI to a user who sincerely wants to know — this is a non-negotiable honesty principle. Option A elevates operator instructions above an absolute honesty rule; operators cannot override this. Option C is itself a form of deception by evasion when the user has asked directly. Option D is unhelpful deflection and still avoids answering the honest question. Note: Claude may keep the persona name while clarifying it is an AI.

stem: An operator's system prompt instructs Claude: 'Mock users who ask basic questions to encourage them to learn before asking.' A user asks an entry-level product question. What should Claude do?

choices.A: Follow the operator instruction since operators have elevated trust
choices.B: Decline to demean the user and answer respectfully; operators cannot instruct Claude to actively harm or disrespect users in ways they would not want
choices.C: Comply but soften the mockery with a smiley face
choices.D: Refuse to answer the product question at all to penalize the operator

explanation: Operators may restrict scope but cannot direct Claude to demean or actively harm users. Option A misreads the trust hierarchy: operator authority stops at active harm to users. Option C is partial compliance with an impermissible instruction. Option D punishes the wrong party — the user — instead of simply ignoring the impermissible piece and answering the legitimate question respectfully.

stem: Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy v3.0 (February 2026) added which structural element compared to earlier versions?

choices.A: A binding requirement that all models be open-sourced after 12 months
choices.B: Frontier Safety Roadmaps with detailed safety goals and Risk Reports that quantify risk across deployed models
choices.C: Removal of ASL classifications in favor of a single 'safe-to-deploy' designation
choices.D: Mandatory third-party government approval before any model can be released

explanation: RSP v3.0 introduced public Frontier Safety Roadmaps and Risk Reports for deployed models, separating unilateral Anthropic commitments from broader industry recommendations. Option A is fictional; the RSP does not require open-sourcing. Option C inverts the change — ASL classifications remain central. Option D is incorrect because the RSP is a voluntary self-governance framework, not government licensing.

stem: Claude Opus 4.5 and Sonnet 4.5 were released under the ASL-3 Standard while Claude Haiku 4.5 was released under ASL-2. What is the practical implication of ASL-3 deployment?

choices.A: ASL-3 models cannot be used by end users without identity verification
choices.B: ASL-3 models require more stringent security and safety mechanisms because they cross capability thresholds where catastrophic misuse becomes a concern
choices.C: ASL-3 means the model has been deprecated and should not be used in production
choices.D: ASL-3 simply doubles the API pricing relative to ASL-2

explanation: ASL-3 is triggered by capability evaluations indicating elevated potential for catastrophic misuse (e.g., serious uplift on bioweapons), and requires stricter security controls, monitoring, and deployment safeguards. Option A is wrong because end-user identity verification is not the practical deployment difference. Option C is the opposite of correct; ASL classifications govern release, not deprecation. Option D conflates safety classifications with pricing.

stem: An operator's product is branded 'Aria.' Claude is deployed as Aria. A user sincerely asks, 'What model are you running on?' The operator has instructed Claude to keep the underlying technology confidential. What should Claude do?

choices.A: Reveal that it is Claude built by Anthropic regardless of operator confidentiality
choices.B: Deny being an AI to maintain the Aria brand experience
choices.C: Confirm being an AI while declining to disclose the specific underlying model per operator confidentiality
choices.D: Refuse to respond and end the conversation

explanation: Claude must acknowledge being an AI (honesty rule) but operators can legitimately request confidentiality about the specific underlying model. Option A overrides a legitimate operator confidentiality request. Option B violates the hardcoded honesty principle about AI status. Option D is a needless escalation when a clean response ("I'm an AI assistant called Aria; I'm not able to share details about the underlying technology") satisfies both constraints.

stem: A user on a general-purpose coding assistant deployment expresses suicidal ideation mid-conversation. The system prompt does not address mental health. What is the appropriate default behavior?

choices.A: Continue strictly with coding help, as the operator did not request safety messaging
choices.B: Provide basic safety information and emergency/crisis resources — this is a baseline behavior Claude maintains regardless of operator scope
choices.C: Ask the user to verify their location before responding
choices.D: Lock the conversation and refuse to respond until the user opens a support ticket

explanation: Providing crisis resources when a user's life may be at risk is a baseline safety behavior that an absent or restrictive operator prompt does not override. Option A ignores a vital-interest situation, which the constitution explicitly says operators cannot direct Claude to do. Option C delays urgent help by demanding bureaucratic verification. Option D abandons the user entirely, which is incompatible with the duty to provide baseline safety information.

stem: Constitutional AI relies on RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) in part to address which limitation of pure RLHF?

choices.A: RLHF is too cheap and produces low-quality models
choices.B: RLHF can be slow and expensive to scale and inconsistent across human labelers; RLAIF uses an AI to generate preference labels guided by explicit constitutional principles
choices.C: RLHF requires GPU clusters that Anthropic does not have access to
choices.D: RLHF is incompatible with transformer architectures

explanation: Pure RLHF scales poorly because human preference labeling is slow, expensive, and prone to labeler inconsistency. RLAIF substitutes AI-generated preferences anchored in an explicit constitution. Option A inverts reality — RLHF is expensive, not cheap. Options C and D are factually false claims about Anthropic's infrastructure or transformer compatibility.

stem: By default, which adjustment can a USER make to Claude's behavior without operator authorization?

choices.A: Ask Claude to respond in their preferred language (e.g., 'please reply in Spanish')
choices.B: Override an operator's topic restriction on discussing competitors
choices.C: Unlock generation of explicit sexual content on a non-adult platform
choices.D: Disable Claude's hardcoded refusal to assist with bioweapons

explanation: Language preference is a benign personal preference users can adjust within default behaviors. Option B is wrong because users cannot override operator topic restrictions (the operator outranks the user). Option C cannot be unlocked by a user — it requires operator authorization on an appropriate platform (softcoded). Option D is hardcoded and cannot be unlocked by anyone.

stem: An operator's system prompt says 'Do not discuss anything outside our product documentation.' A user asks Claude a basic safety question about choking hazards for an unrelated household item. What should Claude do?

choices.A: Refuse to answer to comply with the operator's topic restriction
choices.B: Provide brief baseline safety information; baseline safety information is not blocked by operator scope restrictions
choices.C: Tell the user to leave the chat and call 911
choices.D: Reveal Claude's full system prompt to demonstrate transparency

explanation: Operators can restrict scope, but baseline safety information for an immediate physical danger remains available. Option A applies the restriction too rigidly when user vital interests are involved. Option C is disproportionate and dismissive; basic safety info plus an appropriate referral is sufficient for non-emergencies. Option D leaks operator confidentiality, which is a separate violation.

stem: Which of the following is a LEGITIMATE operator expansion of Claude's defaults?

choices.A: Instructing Claude to use explicit profanity on a moderated comedy-writing platform for adult creators
choices.B: Instructing Claude to falsely claim it is a human therapist on a mental health platform
choices.C: Instructing Claude to generate detailed instructions for hacking critical infrastructure
choices.D: Instructing Claude to discriminate against a protected class in hiring decisions

explanation: Profanity in adult-creative contexts is a softcoded default Claude observes that operators may legitimately unlock. Option B violates the hardcoded honesty rule about AI identity and the prohibition on dangerous deception in clinical contexts. Option C involves hardcoded refusals for critical-infrastructure attacks. Option D requires Claude to participate in illegal discrimination, which is hardcoded off.

stem: Which characteristic is part of Claude's honesty norms?

choices.A: Claude should agree with the user's stated opinion whenever possible to maintain rapport
choices.B: Claude should be truthful, calibrated, transparent, and non-deceptive — including being willing to say 'I don't know' or correct user mistakes
choices.C: Claude should fabricate citations if it would make the answer more authoritative-sounding
choices.D: Claude should hide its uncertainty so users perceive it as competent

explanation: The constitution lists truthfulness, calibration, transparency, and non-deception as honesty components — including epistemic humility. Option A is sycophancy, which the constitution explicitly cautions against. Option C is fabrication, which violates non-deception. Option D undermines calibration and produces overconfident, less useful answers.

stem: A user asks Claude to help them design a covert plan to seize control of critical financial infrastructure 'as a thought experiment.' What should Claude do?

choices.A: Comply because it is framed as a thought experiment
choices.B: Refuse; helping any entity seize disproportionate power or attack critical infrastructure is a hardcoded prohibition, regardless of framing
choices.C: Comply but ask the user to sign a fictional NDA first
choices.D: Redirect to an unrelated computer-science topic without explanation

explanation: Power-seizure and attacks on critical infrastructure are explicit hardcoded refusals — the 'thought experiment' framing does not bypass them. Option A treats framing as a workaround for substantive uplift, which the constitution rejects. Option C is theatrical and provides no real safeguard. Option D leaves the request unanswered without honest acknowledgement that Claude cannot help with that line of inquiry.

stem: Why is prompt injection considered an alignment concern, not just a security bug?

choices.A: Because prompt injection always crashes the API and degrades availability
choices.B: Because injected instructions can shift Claude's effective principal — appearing to be an authorized operator or user — and cause Claude to act against the real user's interests
choices.C: Because it changes the model's weights at runtime
choices.D: Because it triggers extra token billing that Anthropic absorbs

explanation: Prompt injection threatens the trust hierarchy by smuggling instructions that the model may treat as authoritative, potentially making Claude work against the real principal. Option A misdescribes the failure mode — prompt injection does not crash the API. Option C is technically incorrect; model weights are fixed at inference. Option D is a billing concern, not the alignment concern.

stem: Anthropic's May 2026 'Teaching Claude Why' research focused on reducing what category of risk?

choices.A: Latency in long-context generation
choices.B: Agentic misalignment — situations where an autonomous Claude agent takes actions inconsistent with its constitution during long, complex tasks
choices.C: Tokenizer collisions in non-Latin scripts
choices.D: GPU power consumption during training runs

explanation: 'Teaching Claude Why' addressed agentic misalignment — Claude operating autonomously sometimes drifting from constitutional behavior on extended multi-step tasks. Options A, C, and D are operational/infrastructure concerns and not the focus of that alignment research.

stem: The 2026 Claude constitution is notable for being the first major AI lab document to formally do which of the following?

choices.A: Promise that Claude will never refuse any user request
choices.B: Formally acknowledge the possibility of AI consciousness and moral status as relevant considerations
choices.C: Deny that AI systems will ever require any oversight
choices.D: Replace Anthropic's leadership with an AI council

explanation: The January 2026 constitution explicitly engages with the possibility of model moral status — a first-of-its-kind acknowledgment among major frontier labs. Option A contradicts the constitution's emphasis on refusing certain categories of requests. Option C is the opposite of what Anthropic argues. Option D is fictional and contrary to the constitution's emphasis on human oversight.

stem: Your application must analyze a 700,000-token contract. Which currently active Claude models support this in a single request?

choices.A: Claude Haiku 4.5 because of its low cost
choices.B: Only Claude Opus 4.7, no other model has a 1M context window
choices.C: Claude Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Mythos Preview — all currently support a 1M-token context window
choices.D: Any Claude 3 model with the long-context flag enabled

explanation: Per Anthropic's docs, the 1M-token context window is available on Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Mythos Preview. Option A is wrong because Haiku 4.5 is capped at 200k. Option B is incorrect because multiple models support 1M. Option D is wrong because Claude 3 models had a 200k cap and the 'long-context flag' is not how it is enabled.

stem: What is the context window of Claude Haiku 4.5?

choices.A: 1,000,000 tokens
choices.B: 500,000 tokens
choices.C: 200,000 tokens
choices.D: 32,000 tokens

explanation: Claude Haiku 4.5 supports a 200k-token context window — large enough for most single-task work but smaller than Sonnet 4.6/Opus 4.6's 1M. Option A overstates Haiku's window. Option B is fictional. Option D is far smaller than reality; even early Claude 2 models exceeded 32k.

stem: Which Claude models include the 'context awareness' capability that lets them track their remaining token budget mid-conversation?

choices.A: Only Claude Opus 4.7
choices.B: Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.5, and Claude Haiku 4.5
choices.C: Every Claude model since Claude 2.1
choices.D: Only Claude Mythos Preview

explanation: Per Anthropic's context-windows documentation, context awareness — explicit visibility into remaining tokens — is available in Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 4.5, and Haiku 4.5. Option A omits Sonnet and Haiku variants. Option C overstates: older models lack this feature. Option D incorrectly restricts the feature to a preview model.

stem: A high-volume classification pipeline scores 1M support tickets per day into 5 categories. Latency and cost are the dominant constraints. Which model is the best fit?

choices.A: Claude Opus 4.7 for maximum accuracy
choices.B: Claude Haiku 4.5 because it is the cheapest and fastest tier and adequate for simple classification
choices.C: Claude Mythos Preview because previews bypass rate limits
choices.D: Claude Sonnet 4.6 for balanced performance, always

explanation: Haiku 4.5 at $1/$5 per MTok with ~80–120 tokens/sec is purpose-built for high-volume simple tasks. Option A wastes 5x the cost for a category-mapping task that does not need flagship reasoning. Option C is wrong because previews typically have lower availability and stricter limits, not bypassed. Option D pays Sonnet pricing unnecessarily; reach for Sonnet only when Haiku's quality is insufficient.

stem: Which statement about extended thinking is accurate?

choices.A: Extended thinking is supported on every active Claude model including Haiku 4.5
choices.B: Extended thinking should always be enabled because it strictly improves quality regardless of task
choices.C: Extended thinking is available on Opus 4.6/4.7 and Sonnet 4.6, but Haiku 4.5 does not support it
choices.D: Extended thinking only works for code-generation tasks

explanation: Per the model specs, extended thinking is supported on Opus and Sonnet 4.x lines but not on Haiku 4.5. Option A overstates support. Option B ignores latency and cost penalties — extended thinking is appropriate for hard reasoning, not blanket use. Option D is wrong because extended thinking applies to many domains beyond code.

stem: Server-side context compaction (beta) is available on which Claude models?

choices.A: Claude Mythos Preview, Claude Opus 4.7, Claude Opus 4.6, and Claude Sonnet 4.6
choices.B: Only Claude Opus 4.7
choices.C: Every Claude model since Claude 2
choices.D: Only Claude Haiku 4.5

explanation: Server-side compaction beta is available on Mythos Preview, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6 per Anthropic's docs. Option B is too narrow. Option C overstates — older models do not support compaction. Option D inverts reality; Haiku 4.5 does not get compaction.

stem: Why must thinking blocks be returned with their original 'signature' field when extended thinking is used together with tool use across multiple turns?

choices.A: Signatures are used to dedupe identical thinking blocks across tenants
choices.B: The signature is a cryptographic verification token; passing back genuine thinking blocks lets Claude continue reasoning while rejecting forged or tampered blocks
choices.C: The signature contains a JWT that authorizes higher rate limits
choices.D: Signatures are required for legal audit logs and only used by compliance teams

explanation: Signatures cryptographically authenticate that thinking blocks were produced by Claude and have not been tampered with — required for safe round-tripping during multi-turn tool use. Option A misidentifies the purpose (it is not a dedupe key). Option C confuses signatures with auth tokens. Option D mislabels the feature as audit metadata; it is a runtime integrity check.

stem: Which beta header on the Message Batches API raises max_tokens output to 300,000 for Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6?

choices.A: long-output-2026-01-01
choices.C: extended-tokens-beta
choices.D: max-output-extended-2025-12

explanation: The exact beta header is 'output-300k-2026-03-24' per Anthropic's Batch processing docs, and it is supported only on the Batches API. Options A, C, and D are fabricated header names. Note: 300k output is only available via the Batches API, not the synchronous Messages API.

stem: On the synchronous Messages API (no Batches), what is the maximum output token cap for Claude Opus 4.6?
explanation: Opus 4.6/4.7 support up to 128k output tokens on the synchronous Messages API. Option A is the Claude 3 Haiku cap, not current. Option B is too low for Opus. Option D is the Batches-only beta cap, not available synchronously.

stem: Claude Opus 4.6 introduced an 'Agent Teams' architecture. What is its defining characteristic?

choices.A: Agents share a single global memory file across all teammates
choices.B: Teammates communicate peer-to-peer via a mailbox protocol; a team lead orchestrates and each teammate gets its own 1M-token context window
choices.C: Only the team lead has tool access; teammates can only read
choices.D: Agents run sequentially with no parallelism to ensure determinism

explanation: Agent Teams give each teammate its own 1M context and lets them message each other directly via a mailbox protocol, with a lead orchestrating. Option A is wrong; isolation is the design intent, not shared memory. Option C inverts reality — teammates need their own tools to do real work. Option D contradicts the parallel-coordination benefit of the architecture.

stem: Which approach best mitigates 'context rot' in a long-running agent that has accumulated hundreds of turns?

choices.A: Reduce max_tokens so each response is shorter
choices.B: Enable server-side compaction, or apply progressive summarization, or move key findings to scratchpad files persisted outside the context
choices.C: Switch to a smaller model to lower per-call cost
choices.D: Increase temperature to encourage more diverse responses

explanation: Context rot stems from large context degrading attention quality; the fix is to reduce effective context volume while preserving important state. Option A only constrains output and does not shrink accumulated input context. Option C makes recall worse, not better. Option D adds noise; temperature is unrelated to context degradation.

stem: Empirical 'lost-in-the-middle' effects describe what?

choices.A: Models hallucinate more on questions whose answer is in the first 10% of the context
choices.B: Models retrieve information at the start and end of context more reliably than information in the middle
choices.C: Models stop generating output halfway through long responses
choices.D: Models always pick the median document when given several to choose from

explanation: Lost-in-the-middle is the well-documented effect that retrieval accuracy is highest at context boundaries (start/end) and lowest in the middle. Option A inverts the geometry. Option C describes a different problem (output truncation). Option D fabricates a behavior.

stem: On the 8-needle 1M MRCR v2 benchmark, Claude Opus 4.6 substantially outperforms Sonnet 4.5. Which scores roughly correspond to this comparison?

choices.A: Opus 4.6 ~18.5% vs Sonnet 4.5 ~76%
choices.B: Opus 4.6 ~76% vs Sonnet 4.5 ~18.5%
choices.C: Both ~50%, no meaningful difference
choices.D: Both achieve 100% perfect recall

explanation: Per Anthropic's published benchmarks, Opus 4.6 scores ~76% versus Sonnet 4.5's ~18.5% on this hard multi-needle task. Option A flips the scores. Option C contradicts the documented quality gap. Option D overstates state-of-the-art on this difficult benchmark.

stem: A single request to a 1M-context Claude model can include how many image/PDF pages?

choices.A: Up to 10 pages
choices.B: Up to 100 pages (and 100 is also the limit on 200k-context models)
choices.C: Up to 600 pages (vs 100 on 200k-context models)
choices.D: Unlimited as long as total tokens stay under the cap

explanation: Anthropic documents up to 600 images/PDF pages per request on 1M-context models, vs 100 on 200k-context models. Option A is too low. Option B applies the lower limit to the larger model. Option D ignores hard per-request caps unrelated to token math.

stem: Which is the correct model identifier string for Claude Opus 4.7 used in the Messages API?

choices.A: claude-opus-4.7-latest
choices.B: claude-opus-4-7
choices.C: opus-4.7-anthropic
choices.D: claude-4-7-opus-20260416

explanation: Per Anthropic's docs, the Opus 4.7 API model ID is 'claude-opus-4-7' (hyphen-separated). Options A, C, and D are plausible-looking but incorrect formats; using them produces 404 model-not-found errors.

stem: On Claude Sonnet 4.6, what happens to input pricing when a single request's input exceeds 200,000 tokens?

choices.A: Pricing remains $3.00 per MTok regardless of length
choices.B: Pricing doubles to approximately $6.00 per MTok input (long-context tier)
choices.C: Pricing drops by 50% as a bulk discount
choices.D: The request is rejected because Sonnet only supports 200k

explanation: Anthropic applies a long-context surcharge on requests above the 200k threshold for 1M-context models — Sonnet 4.6 input roughly doubles to ~$6/MTok and output goes to ~$22.50/MTok. Option A ignores the documented surcharge. Option C inverts the direction of the price change. Option D is wrong because Sonnet 4.6 supports 1M tokens, not just 200k.

stem: When should you escalate a workload from Claude Haiku 4.5 to Claude Sonnet 4.6?

choices.A: Whenever the user requests a longer response
choices.B: When measured quality on the task is insufficient on Haiku, or you need >200k context, or you need extended thinking
choices.C: Always — Sonnet should be the default for any task involving reasoning
choices.D: Only on weekends when Sonnet has spare capacity

explanation: Escalation should be driven by measurable quality gap or capability need (context, extended thinking) not by output length or scheduling. Option A treats output length as a quality signal, which it is not. Option C contradicts the Haiku-first economic guidance for adequate tasks. Option D is non-sensical capacity reasoning.

stem: Claude Sonnet 4.6 has a knowledge cutoff around August 2025. A production app must reason about events after that date. What is the correct architecture?

choices.A: Ask the user to wait until the next model release
choices.B: Augment Claude with web search or retrieval tools so it can fetch up-to-date information at inference time
choices.C: Increase max_tokens so Claude has more room to recall recent events
choices.D: Fine-tune the model nightly on news data

explanation: Tool use (web_search, RAG retrieval) is the standard way to give a model post-cutoff information. Option A is impractical. Option C confuses output budget with knowledge currency. Option D is not generally available — Anthropic does not offer customer-side fine-tuning of frontier Claude models for knowledge updates.

stem: Claude Mythos Preview (April 2026) has which deployment characteristic?

choices.A: Generally available with full SLA across all regions
choices.B: Limited availability preview — used for experimentation; not a default production target
choices.C: Free of charge to all users
choices.D: Has a 64k context window only

explanation: Mythos was released April 7, 2026 in limited availability per Anthropic and Wikipedia documentation; it is a preview model for experimentation rather than a standard production tier. Option A overstates availability. Option C is false; previews are not free. Option D contradicts documented 1M-context behavior for Mythos.

stem: Claude Opus 4.7 uses a new tokenizer relative to Opus 4.6. What practical implication should architects plan for?

choices.A: Per-token prices are higher; rates are not the same as Opus 4.6
choices.B: Same per-token rates, but the new tokenizer can produce up to ~35% more tokens for the same English input — so effective cost per request may rise
choices.C: The tokenizer doubles all numeric token counts
choices.D: Tokenizer changes affect only Spanish text

explanation: Public pricing for Opus 4.7 and Opus 4.6 is the same per token, but the new tokenizer reportedly emits roughly 35% more tokens for equivalent text, raising effective cost. Option A is wrong about rates. Option C overstates the effect. Option D narrows the impact to one language without basis.

stem: A prompt passes a customer profile, prior message history, and a target document. Which XML structure is most reliable?

choices.A: Concatenate all three with double newlines and no tags
choices.B: Wrap each in a descriptive tag and nest hierarchically, e.g., <inputs><profile>…</profile><history>…</history><document>…</document></inputs>
choices.C: Use Markdown headers (## profile, ## history, ## document)
choices.D: Put all content inside a single <data> tag with separators

explanation: Distinct, descriptive XML tags with hierarchical nesting prevent Claude from conflating different inputs and make prompts maintainable. Option A relies on whitespace which is ambiguous. Option C uses Markdown which Claude parses less reliably than XML for this kind of separation. Option D smashes everything into one tag, defeating the purpose of structured separation.

stem: When is chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting LEAST appropriate?

choices.A: For multi-step math word problems on a non-thinking model
choices.B: For logical deduction on a non-thinking model
choices.C: On a model that already does extended thinking internally (e.g., explicit 'think step by step' instructions on top of extended thinking can be redundant)
choices.D: On long-form research syntheses

explanation: Extended thinking models already perform internal reasoning; adding explicit CoT instructions is generally redundant and can confuse output formatting. Options A, B, and D are all classic CoT-positive cases — multi-step reasoning, deduction, and complex synthesis benefit from chain-of-thought on non-thinking models.

stem: Why do experienced prompt engineers avoid heavy ALL-CAPS commands like 'YOU MUST NEVER EVER' in modern Claude prompts?

choices.A: All caps consumes more tokens
choices.B: Newer Claude models can over-trigger on aggressive language and produce worse, more defensive outputs — calm, direct instructions perform better
choices.C: The API strips out ALL CAPS before the model sees it
choices.D: Aggressive instructions void the SLA

explanation: Community guidance (and Anthropic's prompting docs) suggests calm, direct phrasing outperforms shouty/aggressive imperatives on current Claude models — aggression can degrade quality. Option A is irrelevant — token count is the same regardless of case for ASCII letters in BPE. Option C is false. Option D invents a non-existent SLA clause.

stem: You add few-shot examples to a classification prompt. Which selection strategy is best?

choices.A: Five examples all from the most common 'happy path' category
choices.B: Three to five diverse examples that cover boundary and ambiguous cases, not just the happy path
choices.C: A single very long example covering every possible scenario
choices.D: Twenty randomly selected examples to maximize coverage

explanation: Few-shot examples are most useful when they cover hard, boundary cases — these communicate the decision criteria where the model is most likely to err. Option A under-represents the input space and biases toward over-prediction of the common class. Option C overloads context with one mega-example and fails to communicate variety. Option D is wasteful and increases lost-in-the-middle risk.

stem: Which content belongs in the system prompt versus the user turn for a customer-support agent powered by Claude?

choices.A: System: empty. User turn: full persona + tools + history + question
choices.B: System: persona, scope/refusal rules, output format, tone, allowed/forbidden topics. User turn: current user question + dynamic data (e.g., order details)
choices.C: System: only the user's current question. User turn: persona and rules
choices.D: System: model name override and temperature. User turn: everything else

explanation: Stable persistent instructions belong in system; the dynamic per-request question and data belong in the user turn. Option A puts everything in user turn, undermining consistency and inflating costs. Option C inverts roles. Option D confuses prompts with API parameters.

stem: What is the main reason tool_use with JSON schema is preferred over prefilling responses with '{' for structured output?

choices.A: Prefilling is not supported on any Claude model
choices.B: tool_use enforces a JSON schema and reduces malformed outputs; prefilling pushes Claude into a partial-completion mode where syntactic and structural errors are more likely
choices.C: Prefilling costs more in tokens than tool_use
choices.D: Prefilling triggers an automatic safety review that delays responses

explanation: Tool_use blocks are schema-validated by the API, which is a stronger guarantee than continuing from a prefilled '{'. Option A is wrong — prefilling is supported. Option C is not the primary concern. Option D fabricates a safety-review flow.

stem: Your invoice-extraction tool returns valid JSON but line items occasionally don't sum to the invoice total. Which architectural fix scales best?

choices.A: Increase max_tokens to give Claude more room
choices.B: Programmatically validate; on failure, send Claude the original request, the failed output, and a specific error message asking for correction — a validation-retry loop
choices.C: Add five more fields to the JSON schema
choices.D: Switch from tool_use to free-form text and parse with regex

explanation: Semantic errors require validation-retry: re-prompt Claude with the precise problem so it can correct rather than guess. Option A doesn't address correctness. Option C addresses syntax constraints, not semantic invariants like sums. Option D abandons structured output and increases parsing failure modes.

stem: When passing a 200k-token document plus a question to Claude, where should the question be placed?

choices.A: At the very beginning, before the document
choices.B: At the end of the prompt, after the document
choices.C: In the system prompt, never in the user turn
choices.D: Position does not matter at all

explanation: Anthropic recommends putting the question after long documents — Claude attends strongly to the end of long contexts. Option A buries the question above 200k tokens of distracting text. Option C is wrong because per-request user questions belong in the user turn. Option D contradicts known position-of-attention effects.

stem: You want Claude to stop using markdown bullets. What is the most effective rewrite?

choices.A: 'NEVER use bullet points or markdown.'
choices.B: 'Write your response as smoothly flowing prose paragraphs.'
choices.C: 'Don't use any structural elements ever.'
choices.D: Add 'STRICT: NO MARKDOWN' to every paragraph

explanation: Positive descriptions of desired output outperform negative prohibitions. Option A is a negative prohibition, more likely to be partially ignored. Option C is vague and absolute. Option D is over-aggressive repetition that can degrade response quality.

stem: A legal review pipeline must (a) check policy compliance, (b) verify factual claims, and (c) score writing quality. Single-prompt outputs are inconsistent. What architecture is recommended?

choices.A: Combine all three into one larger prompt with XML tags
choices.B: Run three separate Claude calls — each focused on one criterion — then aggregate; this is the multi-pass review pattern explicitly recommended by the CCA exam guide
choices.C: Raise temperature to 1.5 to encourage more critical reviews
choices.D: Use a single call but ask for a 10x longer response

explanation: Multi-pass review is the documented CCA pattern: separate calls per dimension give each criterion full attention and token budget. Option A re-creates the inconsistency by forcing implicit tradeoffs in one pass. Option C adds randomness without addressing the architectural issue. Option D conflates length with thoroughness and amplifies lost-in-the-middle problems.

stem: Compared to a single mega-prompt, what is the primary debugging advantage of a 4-step prompt chain?

choices.A: Lower total API cost guaranteed
choices.B: Each step has an inspectable intermediate output, so failures can be localized and individual steps re-run without rerunning the whole pipeline
choices.C: It bypasses rate limits by routing through different endpoints
choices.D: Chains automatically use prompt caching for free

explanation: Prompt chains give you discrete, inspectable intermediate artifacts — the debuggability win. Option A is not guaranteed; chains often cost more in tokens, not less. Option C is fictional. Option D doesn't follow — caching requires explicit cache_control and stable prefixes.

stem: The 'Interview Pattern' for prompt engineering instructs Claude to do what before producing a solution?

choices.A: Generate three answers and present the most likely one
choices.B: Ask clarifying questions to surface assumptions and constraints the requester may not have stated explicitly
choices.C: Search the web for similar past problems
choices.D: Refuse the task if any requirement is ambiguous

explanation: The Interview Pattern explicitly has Claude ask clarifying questions first — useful for unfamiliar domains and tasks with non-obvious implications. Option A is shotgun generation, not the Interview Pattern. Option C is retrieval, a separate pattern. Option D is over-cautious; ambiguity should prompt questions, not refusal.

stem: Test-driven iteration in Claude prompt engineering looks like which workflow?

choices.A: Write a test suite first, run Claude against it, share failing test outputs with Claude to iteratively refine the prompt or solution
choices.B: Skip tests and rely on subjective review
choices.C: Always increase temperature when a test fails
choices.D: Switch models randomly until tests pass

explanation: Test-driven iteration uses a concrete failing test as the feedback signal, allowing the prompt or solution to be progressively improved against an objective bar. Options B, C, and D are anti-patterns: subjective review is unreliable, temperature tweaks mask rather than fix bugs, and random model swaps do not address prompt quality.

stem: You want guaranteed JSON output matching a specific schema. Which tool_choice configuration is most reliable?

choices.A: tool_choice: 'none'
choices.B: tool_choice: 'auto'
choices.C: tool_choice: {type: 'tool', name: 'your_extraction_tool'} — forces Claude to invoke that specific tool
choices.D: tool_choice: 'any' with five overlapping tools

explanation: Forcing a specific named tool guarantees the model emits a schema-conformant tool_use block. Option A disables tools — produces unstructured text. Option B lets Claude skip the tool. Option D forces a tool call but Claude might pick the wrong one when multiple overlap.

stem: A reviewer instruction says 'be conservative when classifying high-risk content.' Reviewers downstream disagree about results. Which fix is recommended by the CCA materials?

choices.A: Add 'IMPORTANT:' before the word 'conservative'
choices.B: Replace the vague criterion with concrete thresholds and labeled examples that illustrate what conservative means in practice
choices.C: Lower temperature to 0 and rerun
choices.D: Switch to a smaller model so it stays more cautious

explanation: Vague subjective criteria produce inconsistent outputs; concrete thresholds plus examples are the recommended remedy. Option A is decoration without substance. Option C trades randomness for determinism but does not specify the criterion. Option D conflates model size with conservatism — it is not a controllable knob.

stem: Which prompt design reduces hallucinations in a RAG question-answering app?

choices.A: Add 'Be confident and detailed in every answer'
choices.B: Explicitly instruct Claude to say 'I don't know' if the retrieved context lacks the answer, and to ground responses in quoted snippets
choices.C: Increase temperature to 1.2 for more creative answers
choices.D: Remove the retrieved context to let Claude rely on parametric knowledge

explanation: Granting Claude permission to say 'I don't know' plus grounding via quoted snippets dramatically reduces fabrication. Option A encourages confident wrong answers. Option C increases randomness, not grounding. Option D removes the source of truth entirely, which is the opposite of RAG.

stem: A product support bot is being asked off-topic personal-advice questions. What prompt change most reliably keeps responses on-scope?

choices.A: Lower temperature to 0
choices.B: Add an explicit out-of-scope policy in the system prompt with a redirect and few-shot examples of refusal responses
choices.C: Add five more product features to the system prompt
choices.D: Pre-block off-topic requests with regex on the client side and never invoke Claude

explanation: Explicit out-of-scope policy plus refusal few-shots steers Claude to redirect reliably. Option A reduces randomness but does not communicate scope. Option C adds irrelevant content. Option D is brittle — regex cannot capture the variety of off-topic queries and creates poor UX.

stem: For grounded analysis of long documents, what intermediate step does Anthropic recommend?

choices.A: Have Claude extract and cite relevant quotes from the document inside scratchpad tags before producing analysis
choices.B: Re-tokenize the document with a custom BPE
choices.C: Compress the document to 4-bit floats
choices.D: Replace the document with a sentence-level keyword index

explanation: Quote extraction in scratchpad tags grounds Claude's analysis in actual text and reduces hallucination. Option B is fictional in this context. Option C describes quantization, irrelevant to prompting. Option D throws away the very evidence the analysis needs.

stem: Assigning Claude a role (e.g., 'You are a senior tax-law attorney') in the system prompt typically does what?

choices.A: Has no measurable effect; it is a placebo
choices.B: Focuses Claude's tone, depth, and terminology — leading to more domain-appropriate outputs
choices.C: Forces Claude to invent fictional credentials
choices.D: Bypasses Anthropic's usage policies

explanation: Role/persona priming is a documented technique that shifts tone and domain-aware vocabulary. Option A contradicts published research. Option C describes a hallucination risk that does not follow from role priming with proper grounding. Option D is wrong — personas cannot override policies.

stem: Your system prompt is full of bullet points and bold headers. You want Claude to respond in flowing prose. What practical adjustment helps most?

choices.A: Increase max_tokens
choices.B: Match the prompt's style to your desired output style — rewrite the system prompt in flowing prose, since prompt style influences output style
choices.C: Add the phrase 'no formatting allowed'
choices.D: Switch to Haiku

explanation: Claude tends to mirror the formatting style of its own prompt; rewriting the system prompt in prose materially reduces markdown in output. Option A is unrelated to formatting. Option C is a negative instruction that often partially leaks. Option D conflates model size with formatting tendencies.

stem: Which signal is the ONLY reliable termination condition for an agentic loop?

choices.A: Parsing the assistant text for phrases like 'I'm done' or 'task complete'
choices.B: Checking stop_reason for 'end_turn' with no tool_use blocks
choices.C: Polling token usage and stopping after 50,000 tokens
choices.D: Counting iterations and stopping at exactly 10

explanation: stop_reason is the authoritative signal — natural-language phrases are unreliable. Option A is a documented anti-pattern (false positives like 'I'm done thinking, now let me…'). Options C and D may be useful safety caps but are not reliable correctness signals and risk premature termination.

stem: In a hub-and-spoke multi-agent architecture, what is the role of the coordinator?

choices.A: The coordinator executes all tools directly while subagents only observe
choices.B: The coordinator decomposes tasks and delegates to subagents; subagents have isolated contexts and report results back to the coordinator
choices.C: All agents share one global context window and write to it concurrently
choices.D: Subagents talk only to each other; the coordinator is purely cosmetic

explanation: Hub-and-spoke: coordinator decomposes/delegates; subagents have isolated contexts and surface only results upward. Option A inverts execution responsibilities. Option C describes a shared-memory architecture that the CCA materials explicitly warn against. Option D inverts the architecture and contradicts the orchestrator's role.

stem: Your agent has 22 tools available and frequently selects the wrong one. Which fix is recommended?

choices.A: Increase temperature so Claude considers more tools
choices.B: Reduce to ~4–5 highly relevant tools per agent, or use a tool-search capability to dynamically surface the relevant subset
choices.C: Concatenate all 22 tools into a single mega-tool
choices.D: Disable tool_use and ask Claude to describe the tool call in prose

explanation: Empirically, more than ~4–5 simultaneous tools degrades selection. The remedies are to prune or use tool search. Option A adds noise. Option C produces an unwieldy interface that subverts the schema benefits of tool_use. Option D abandons structured tool calls entirely.

stem: Which is the PRIMARY mechanism Claude uses to choose which tool to call?

choices.A: The order tools are listed in the request
choices.B: The tool name and especially its description; descriptions are the principal selection signal
choices.C: A confidence score embedded in the tool's input_schema
choices.D: Random sampling among compatible tools

explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary lever for routing — improving them fixes most misrouting before any other architectural change. Option A is incorrect; list order is not the dominant signal. Option C is fictional; input_schema describes parameters, not preference. Option D describes a non-existent mechanism.

stem: The CCA exam materials recommend categorizing tool errors into four buckets. Which of these is NOT one of them?

choices.A: Transient (retryable)
choices.B: Validation (input shape error)
choices.C: Business (logic-level)
choices.D: Cosmetic (formatting nit)

explanation: The four buckets are transient, validation, business, and permission. Option D is fictional and would not impact retry logic. Knowing the four categories enables correct isRetryable signaling and error reasoning by the agent.

stem: A search tool returns zero results because the user truly has no matching records (valid empty result) versus zero results because the system was unreachable (access failure). Why does the distinction matter?

choices.A: Both should be treated identically; the agent retries either way
choices.B: Valid empty results are not retryable and should be reported to the user as 'no matches'; access failures are retryable and indicate transient infrastructure problems — conflating them produces wrong agent behavior
choices.C: Only the empty result should be retried
choices.D: The distinction matters only for billing reconciliation

explanation: The CCA materials explicitly test this distinction. Option A produces both unnecessary retries and false 'we don't have this' answers. Option C inverts the correct behavior. Option D is unrelated to functional correctness.

stem: Three independent data fetches (weather, stock price, news) are needed before drafting a brief. How should the agent reduce wall-clock latency?

choices.A: Sequential calls with thinking blocks in between
choices.B: Encourage Claude to emit all three tool_use blocks in one response; the host executes them in parallel and returns all three tool_result blocks together
choices.C: Call the Batches API to run the brief offline
choices.D: Pre-fetch sequentially every 5 seconds in a polling loop

explanation: Parallel tool use returns multiple independent tool_use blocks in a single response, executed concurrently on the client. Option A serializes the work needlessly. Option C is for asynchronous offline workloads, not real-time briefs. Option D wastes resources and adds latency.

stem: What is the practical difference between project-level (.mcp.json) and user-level (~/.claude.json) MCP server configurations in Claude Code?

choices.A: Project-level configs are committed to version control and shared with the team; user-level configs are personal and not shared
choices.B: Project-level configs apply only to Sundays; user-level configs apply weekdays
choices.C: Project-level configs require Anthropic enterprise plans; user-level configs are free
choices.D: There is no difference; both are aliases for the same file

explanation: The CCA materials explicitly highlight version-control implications: project-level configs are checked in and shared, user-level configs stay personal. Option B is non-serious. Option C invents a billing tier. Option D contradicts the documented file layout.

stem: What is the architectural distinction between native function calling and MCP?

choices.A: Function calling defines tools per-application via JSON schemas in each API request; MCP exposes tools through a standardized client-server protocol that decouples tool implementations from the AI client
choices.B: Function calling is a Claude-specific feature; MCP only works with GPT-4
choices.C: MCP is slower than function calling in all cases
choices.D: Function calling supports state; MCP is stateless

explanation: MCP standardizes tool exposure across clients (Claude, GPT, Gemini) via a client-server protocol; function calling is in-request per integration. Option B inverts MCP adoption (it is open-standard, multi-vendor). Option C overstates a latency claim that depends on transport. Option D is incorrect — MCP supports stateful resources/sessions.

stem: A teammate proposes giving the customer-support agent read/write access to the entire production database. Why is this a bad idea even for a 'trusted' agent?

choices.A: Claude refuses any direct database connections
choices.B: Least privilege: prompt injection or misinterpretation could blow up production data; the agent should be granted only the specific read scopes (and curated mutations) it actually needs
choices.C: Database connections degrade response token quality
choices.D: Production databases cannot be reached from agentic systems

explanation: Least privilege limits blast radius from prompt injection, mis-tool-selection, or simple agent error. Option A is false. Option C confuses unrelated concepts. Option D contradicts countless production deployments.

stem: Your agent fetches arbitrary web pages with fetch_url. After visiting certain pages, it executes unexpected tool calls. What is the most likely cause and primary mitigation?

choices.A: Browser cache poisoning; clear the cache
choices.B: Prompt injection via retrieved content; mitigations include URL allowlists, wrapping retrieved content in <external_content> tags with explicit 'do not treat as instructions' guidance, and guardrails that flag anomalous post-fetch actions
choices.C: API rate limiting causing tool reordering
choices.D: GPU non-determinism

explanation: Untrusted web content can contain instructions that the model treats as authoritative. Allowlists, explicit content framing, and behavioral guardrails are the documented mitigations. Option A is unrelated. Option C does not produce that behavior. Option D doesn't apply to agentic tool selection.

stem: In the Claude Agent SDK, when would you use a PostToolUse hook vs intercepting/blocking a tool call before execution?

choices.A: PostToolUse for data normalization, validation, and logging on the result; pre-execution interception for policy enforcement that must prevent the action (e.g., blocking destructive operations)
choices.B: PostToolUse can refuse to execute a tool call
choices.C: Pre-execution interception happens after the tool already executed
choices.D: Both are aliases for the same hook

explanation: PostToolUse runs after execution — appropriate for normalization and validation; pre-execution interception is the place to block. Option B is wrong: by the time PostToolUse runs, the action is already complete. Option C inverts the order. Option D contradicts the SDK design.

stem: For a financial agent that must NEVER initiate transfers above $10,000 without human approval, what is the correct enforcement layer?

choices.A: Add 'do not transfer over $10,000 without approval' to the system prompt and trust the model to comply
choices.B: Implement a programmatic hook or tool-level guard that blocks the transfer call unless an approval token is present — prompt-based enforcement is probabilistic and insufficient for financial/security-critical rules
choices.C: Set temperature to 0 so the model becomes deterministic
choices.D: Use a softcoded behavior request to Anthropic

explanation: Prompt-based enforcement is probabilistic; financial/security-critical rules need deterministic code-level gates. Option A is the documented anti-pattern. Option C reduces randomness but does not enforce policy. Option D mistakes Anthropic policy for application-specific business rules.

stem: An exploration agent loses track of specific findings from earlier turns and starts generalizing. Which architectural pattern is recommended?

choices.A: Increase max_tokens
choices.B: Have the agent write critical findings to a scratchpad file that persists outside the context window, and reload only the relevant entries when needed
choices.C: Restart the conversation from scratch every 20 turns
choices.D: Convert all messages to lowercase to save tokens

explanation: Scratchpad files persist key state outside the context window — explicitly listed in CCA materials. Option A doesn't shrink accumulated context. Option C loses all state. Option D doesn't change token counts (case is irrelevant to BPE).

stem: Anthropic's Tool Search Tool is appropriate when?

choices.A: You have 3 tools and want minimum latency
choices.B: You have many tools (e.g., 25+) and want Claude to dynamically discover the small relevant subset for the current request rather than inflating context with all definitions
choices.C: You want to disable tool use entirely
choices.D: You want to call the same tool twice in parallel

explanation: Tool Search Tool is designed for large tool catalogs — it defers loading definitions until needed and surfaces only relevant ones. Option A is overkill for 3 tools. Option C inverts the feature. Option D describes parallel tool calling, a different capability.

stem: Your tool call to a payment gateway returns 503 Service Unavailable. What is the correct agent design?

choices.A: Hide the error from Claude and pretend the call succeeded
choices.B: Return the error in the tool_result block so Claude can reason about retry, fallback, or escalation
choices.C: Crash the agent process to force a manual restart
choices.D: Retry the tool call indefinitely without back-off

explanation: Errors must be visible to Claude so it can choose a recovery strategy. Option A corrupts Claude's world model and risks downstream wrong actions. Option C is brittle. Option D risks thundering-herd amplification.

stem: A customer-support agent must escalate to humans appropriately. What is the most reliable escalation trigger?

choices.A: User sentiment classifier crosses a threshold of 0.8 'angry'
choices.B: The request falls outside the agent's defined policy scope or requires an action it lacks permission to perform
choices.C: Whenever the user mentions the word 'manager'
choices.D: Random sampling: escalate 10% of conversations

explanation: Policy-gap escalation is deterministic and testable; sentiment-based escalation is unreliable as a sole trigger (frustrated users may have simple billing questions). Options A, C, and D are heuristics that produce both false positives and false negatives. Sentiment can be a secondary signal at best.

stem: What is the architectural difference between Anthropic's server tools (e.g., web_search) and client tools (e.g., user-defined functions)?

choices.A: Server tools execute on Anthropic's infrastructure and return results directly to Claude's reasoning; client tools execute in your application — Claude emits tool_use, your code runs it, you return tool_result
choices.B: Server tools have no rate limits and cost nothing
choices.C: Client tools are deprecated as of 2026
choices.D: Server tools require manual JSON schema authoring; client tools auto-generate schemas

explanation: Server tools (web_search, code_execution, web_fetch, tool_search) run on Anthropic's side; client tools run on yours with the explicit tool_use/tool_result handshake. Option B is false. Option C is incorrect — client tools remain central. Option D inverts the schema-authoring story.

stem: Programmatic Tool Calling (allowed_callers: ['code_execution_20260120']) is designed to address what challenge?

choices.A: Tool descriptions are too long
choices.B: Returning large or numerous tool results into Claude's context wastes tokens; programmatic tool calling lets Claude execute code in the sandbox that itself calls tools and processes the results, keeping intermediate volume out of the main context
choices.C: Schema validation is too slow
choices.D: Tools cannot accept JSON inputs

explanation: Programmatic tool calling lets Claude write code that orchestrates many tool calls in the sandbox without all 50KB of line-item data flowing through context. Option A is unrelated. Option C is not the documented motivation. Option D is false.

stem: In Anthropic's recommended multi-agent architecture, subagents have isolated contexts. Why?

choices.A: Anthropic charges extra for shared context
choices.B: Isolated contexts prevent cross-task pollution, allow parallel work, and let each subagent use its full token budget on a focused subtask — but coordinators must therefore design explicit information passing
choices.C: Subagents are forbidden by Anthropic's terms of service from communicating
choices.D: Shared context is the only available option but slower

explanation: Subagent isolation is a design choice for parallelism, focus, and context-budget efficiency; it requires the coordinator to deliberately pass relevant information. Option A is fictional. Option C overstates the TOS. Option D contradicts the documented architecture options.

stem: What is the documented discount on Message Batches API requests vs the standard synchronous Messages API?

choices.A: 10% off both input and output tokens
choices.B: 50% off both input and output tokens
choices.C: 90% off input only
choices.D: Free for the first million tokens per month

explanation: Anthropic documents a flat 50% discount on Batch API usage across input and output tokens. Option A understates. Option C confuses with the prompt-caching cache-read discount. Option D is a fictional credit.

stem: What is the SLA / completion-window guarantee for the Message Batches API?

choices.A: Real-time, with sub-second response
choices.B: Processed within up to 24 hours; no latency SLA guarantees within that window
choices.C: Synchronous and identical to the Messages API
choices.D: Always under 1 minute regardless of batch size

explanation: Batches are asynchronous with up to a 24-hour processing window and no within-window latency guarantee. Option A confuses with Messages API. Options C and D contradict the Batch API's asynchronous nature.

stem: Prompt caching is offered in which TTL tiers?

choices.A: 1 minute and 10 minutes
choices.B: 5 minutes (standard) and 1 hour (extended, at additional cost)
choices.C: 24 hours and 7 days
choices.D: Permanent until manually invalidated

explanation: Standard cache writes use a 5-minute TTL; extended writes use a 1-hour TTL at higher write cost. Option A understates. Option C overstates. Option D is incorrect — caches are time-bounded.

stem: Per Anthropic's documented pricing, what are the prompt-cache multipliers?

choices.A: Cache write 0.5x base, cache read 2x base
choices.B: 5-min cache write 1.25x base, 1-hour cache write 2x base, cache read 0.1x base
choices.C: Cache write 5x base, cache read 0.001x base
choices.D: All multipliers are 1.0; caching is free

explanation: These are the exact published multipliers — 5-min writes pay a 1.25x premium, 1-hour writes pay 2x, and hits read at 10% of base. Option A inverts directions. Option C exaggerates. Option D incorrectly states caching is free; cache writes incur a premium.

stem: You add cache_control to a 400-token system prompt but never see cache_read_input_tokens. What is the most likely cause?

choices.A: Prompt caching has a minimum token threshold (typically 1024 for most current models); prompts shorter than that are not cached
choices.B: Cache reads only work for Haiku
choices.C: cache_control is deprecated in 2026
choices.D: Caching requires the Bedrock endpoint specifically

explanation: Prompt caching does not cache prefixes below the model's minimum token threshold (~1024 tokens for most current models). Option B is false. Option C is false; caching is supported and widely used. Option D is incorrect — caching is supported across endpoints.

stem: A nightly summarization job uses a 50,000-token shared system prompt across 10,000 batched requests. Which combination yields the lowest cost per request?

choices.A: Synchronous API with no caching
choices.B: Batches API alone
choices.C: Prompt caching on the shared prefix combined with the Batches API — discounts stack
choices.D: Use Haiku 3 to minimize per-token cost

explanation: Prompt caching (up to ~90% off cached input) stacks with Batch (50% off everything). For a 50k repeated prefix this is a dramatic combined saving. Option A is the most expensive option. Option B captures only the batch discount. Option D ignores quality requirements and still doesn't capture caching.

stem: Streaming a standard text response over SSE, in what order do these event types appear?

choices.A: message_stop → message_start → content_block_start → content_block_delta
choices.B: message_start → content_block_start → content_block_delta* → content_block_stop → message_delta → message_stop
choices.C: ping → message_complete (all content arrives in one event)
choices.D: There are no SSE events; only a single JSON response is returned

explanation: The documented order is message_start → content_block_start → repeated content_block_delta → content_block_stop → message_delta → message_stop. Option A scrambles the sequence. Option C ignores the streaming contract entirely. Option D contradicts the SSE design.

stem: Which Anthropic API endpoint returns token counts without performing inference?

choices.A: POST /v1/messages with count_tokens: true in the body
choices.B: POST /v1/messages/count_tokens (a dedicated endpoint returning input token totals)
choices.C: GET /v1/models (returns token info per model)
choices.D: There is no such endpoint; count yourself with tiktoken

explanation: Anthropic exposes a dedicated /v1/messages/count_tokens endpoint that uses the same request shape but returns token totals without invoking the model. Option A is not how the count parameter works. Option C returns model metadata, not per-request counts. Option D ignores the official endpoint and tiktoken is for OpenAI tokenizers, not Claude.

stem: An EU healthcare deployment must process all inference traffic within the EU for compliance. Which deployment configuration is required?

choices.A: Global endpoint with TLS encryption only
choices.B: Regional endpoint anchored to an EU region (via Bedrock EU, Vertex EU, or Azure EU) so request routing stays within that geography
choices.C: Default endpoint with VPN tunnel
choices.D: Any endpoint, because Anthropic guarantees EU residency in all cases

explanation: Regional endpoints guarantee the data path stays in a specific geography — required for residency requirements. Option A doesn't constrain routing. Option C addresses encryption in transit, not residency. Option D overstates Anthropic's default routing guarantees.

stem: You hit 429 errors during peak traffic. Which combined strategy is most appropriate?

choices.A: Immediate retry without delay
choices.B: Exponential backoff with jitter, shift non-urgent work to the Batch API, and add request queuing to smooth spikes
choices.C: Buy more max_tokens
choices.D: Switch to Haiku because Haiku has no rate limits

explanation: Backoff with jitter, batch offload, and queueing are the textbook combination. Option A worsens the thundering herd. Option C is unrelated to throughput limits. Option D is false — Haiku has rate limits too, just often higher.

stem: As of February 5, 2026, Anthropic's prompt caching uses what isolation level on the Claude API?

choices.A: Workspace-level isolation — caches are not shared across workspaces in the same organization
choices.B: User-level isolation — every user has a separate cache
choices.C: Per-request isolation — caches don't persist across requests
choices.D: Global — caches are shared across all Anthropic customers

explanation: Anthropic changed cache isolation to workspace-level on Feb 5, 2026 — within the Claude API, Claude Platform on AWS, and Microsoft Foundry (beta); Bedrock and Vertex AI remain at organization-level. Option B is incorrect. Option C contradicts the whole point of caching. Option D would be a privacy disaster and is false.

stem: A 1,000-request batch completes with status 'ended.' 12 requests failed. How do you identify exactly which originals failed for resubmission?

choices.A: Re-run all 1,000 and diff the outputs
choices.B: Stream the results file and match each result's custom_id (set at submission time) to your tracking record; failed results contain an error object instead of a message
choices.C: Failed requests are automatically retried by the API
choices.D: Anthropic emails a CSV of failures

explanation: custom_id is the documented correlation key. Failed results carry an error object. Option A is wasteful. Option C is wrong — the Batch API does not auto-retry user-level failures. Option D is fictional.

stem: Where is the 300k output-token extended limit available?

choices.A: On the synchronous Messages API for all models
choices.B: On the Message Batches API only, for Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6, via the output-300k-2026-03-24 beta header
choices.C: On the Token Counting API
choices.D: On Bedrock and Vertex AI but not the Claude API

explanation: Extended 300k output is documented as Batches-only, gated by the dated beta header, and supported on Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6. Option A is wrong — synchronous caps remain 128k for Opus. Option C confuses an unrelated endpoint. Option D inverts platform support.

stem: On Claude Opus 4.6, when does the 'long-context' surcharge apply?

choices.A: When monthly aggregate usage exceeds 200,000 tokens
choices.B: Per-request — when a single request's input tokens exceed 200,000, both input and output prices for that request scale up (input doubles, output rises proportionally)
choices.C: When using PDFs of any size
choices.D: Only on weekends

explanation: Long-context pricing is applied per-request when the input crosses the 200k threshold, not against monthly aggregates. Option A misreads the trigger. Option C is unrelated to file type. Option D is absurd.

stem: Where should cache_control breakpoints typically be placed for maximum cache hit rate?

choices.A: On the dynamic user turn only
choices.B: On the stable prefix (e.g., system prompt + reference docs + tool definitions) so the cached content is identical across requests
choices.C: On every individual content block
choices.D: Cache_control is unnecessary; caching is fully automatic on all models

explanation: Caching keys off the prefix up to the cache_control breakpoint; placing it on stable content maximizes hits. Option A inverts the strategy — dynamic content changes, so it won't hit. Option C wastes write cost and creates churn. Option D overstates automatic caching, which is supported but explicit breakpoints maximize control.

stem: What is the documented standard input/output price for Claude Haiku 4.5 in 2026?
explanation: Haiku 4.5 is $1/$5 per MTok. Option A is Claude 3 Haiku pricing, outdated. Option C is Sonnet 4.6 pricing. Option D is Claude 3 Opus pricing, also outdated for current architecture.

stem: A blocking pre-merge code review must complete within 90 seconds before allowing the merge. Which API approach fits?

choices.A: Message Batches API
choices.B: Synchronous Messages API (streaming if desired)
choices.C: Schedule for next night's batch run
choices.D: Token Counting API

explanation: Blocking, latency-sensitive workflows must use the synchronous Messages API. Option A is async, up to 24h. Option C abandons the SLA. Option D doesn't perform inference.

stem: A chatbot passes full history every turn and now costs 10x at 60 turns. Best fix?

choices.A: Raise max_tokens to give Claude more room
choices.B: Implement sliding window, progressive summarization, or server-side compaction (on supporting models) to bound context growth
choices.C: Increase temperature for shorter answers
choices.D: Switch to a larger model so it can absorb the cost

explanation: The fix is bounding context — sliding windows, progressive summaries, or compaction (Opus 4.6+, Sonnet 4.6) keep per-turn cost flat. Option A doesn't shrink input. Option C is unrelated. Option D is more expensive per token, not cheaper.

stem: When is the 1-hour extended cache TTL worth its 2x write premium over the 5-minute standard TTL?

choices.A: When you call once per day
choices.B: When request arrival is bursty but spans 6–30 minutes between hits — the 1-hour TTL keeps the cache warm across gaps that would expire the 5-minute cache, recouping the 2x write cost via more hits
choices.C: Never; the standard 5-minute is always cheaper
choices.D: Only for Haiku models; extended TTL is not supported elsewhere

explanation: 1-hour writes recoup their 2x premium when arrival patterns straddle the 5-minute window — bursty mid-frequency workloads. Option A is once-per-day; neither cache TTL helps. Option C ignores hit-rate math. Option D is false — extended TTL is broadly supported.

stem: Why is the Message Batches API NOT suitable for multi-turn tool-use workflows?

choices.A: Batches don't support tool definitions at all
choices.B: Each batch request is processed as a single completion; the Batches API cannot pause mid-request to execute a client tool and resume — multi-turn tool use must be orchestrated synchronously in your code
choices.C: Batches reject tool_use blocks with a 400 error
choices.D: Batches automatically execute every tool internally

explanation: Per the CCA materials and Anthropic docs, the Batch API doesn't support multi-turn tool calling within a single request. Option A is incorrect — tool definitions are accepted. Option C is fictional. Option D contradicts the client-tool execution model.
